data.title: Guzman y Gomez (ASX:GYG) Is Up 5.7% After Strong Q3 Sales And Drive-Thru Expansion Plans
data.description:
• In the third quarter ended March 31, 2026, Guzman y Gomez Limited reported network sales of A$345.9 million, up from A$289.5 million a year earlier, and confirmed it remains on track to open 32 new Australian restaurants in FY26, including 23 drive-thrus.
  • This combination of solid sales momentum and a heavy tilt toward new drive-thru locations underscores how GYG is leaning into higher-throughput formats to support its growth ambitions.
  • We’ll now consider how this stronger quarterly sales performance and accelerated Australian drive-thru rollout affects Guzman y Gomez’s broader investment narrative.

Guzman y Gomez Investment Narrative Recap

To own Guzman y Gomez, you need to believe its fast-casual model can keep scaling without eroding unit economics, especially as it pushes toward 1,000 Australian stores and tests the US. The latest quarter’s A$345.9 million in network sales and confirmation of 32 new FY26 openings (mostly drive thrus) support the near term growth catalyst, but they also sharpen the risk that rapid expansion eventually pressures same store sales if demand fails to keep pace.

The most relevant recent announcement alongside this trading update is the A$100 million on-market buyback running through October 2026. Set against strong sales and a heavier mix of higher throughput drive thrus, this capital return program adds another layer to the story: management is committing cash to shareholders while still funding a dense Australian rollout, which could amplify both the upside from execution and the downside if expansion or margins stumble.

Yet this acceleration also raises fresh questions about how much store growth the Australian market can absorb before saturation becomes a real issue investors should be aware of...

data.title: The Bull Case For Leidos Holdings (LDOS) Could Change Following Security Screening JV With Altaris And Analogic
data.description:
• In mid-April 2026, Leidos and investment firm Altaris announced a joint venture that combines Leidos’ Security Enterprise Solutions business with Altaris-owned Analogic to form a U.S.-based security screening enterprise focused on airports, borders, and critical infrastructure worldwide.
  • This move shifts roughly 1,500 employees and an expected US$625 million in 2026 revenue into a privately held company under the Analogic brand, allowing Leidos to concentrate capital on areas prioritized in its NorthStar 2030 plan while retaining a meaningful ownership stake.
  • We’ll now examine how transferring the Security Enterprise Solutions unit into the Altaris-led joint venture could reshape Leidos’ longer-term investment narrative.

Leidos Holdings Investment Narrative Recap

To own Leidos, you need to be comfortable with a federally focused contractor that is trying to tilt more toward higher margin, technology driven work. The Altaris joint venture looks incremental rather than transformational in the very near term, with the key short term catalyst still tied to executing large cloud and AI modernization contracts, while the biggest risk remains heavy exposure to shifting U.S. government spending priorities.

The Altaris Analogic joint venture matters most because it lines up with Leidos’ NorthStar 2030 focus on digital modernization and security tech, effectively moving lower growth hardware centric screening into a separate vehicle while preserving a minority stake. That context sits alongside the ENTRUST acquisition, which is aimed at deepening Leidos’ role in cybersecurity and digital identity, both central to the contract pipeline investors are watching.

data.title: Why Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) Is Up 5.5% After 52-Week REZOLVE-AA Rezpegaldesleukin Data Release And What's Next
data.description:
• Nektar Therapeutics recently hosted an investor call to review 52-week topline results from the 16-week extension of its ongoing Phase 2b REZOLVE-AA trial of investigational rezpegaldesleukin, a regulatory T-cell proliferator for severe-to-very-severe alopecia areata, with detailed materials posted on the company’s website.
  • This readout matters because it extends the evidence base for rezpegaldesleukin in a high-need autoimmune hair loss indication, potentially informing next steps alongside its atopic dermatitis program.
  • We’ll now explore how the new 52-week REZOLVE-AA data for rezpegaldesleukin could reshape Nektar’s REZPEG-centered investment narrative.

Uncover the next big thing with 26 elite penny stocks that balance risk and reward.

Advertisement

Nektar Therapeutics Investment Narrative Recap

To own Nektar today, you have to believe rezpegaldesleukin can translate its mid‑stage autoimmune data into a viable, de‑risked late‑stage franchise before cash pressures and dilution erode value. The new 52‑week REZOLVE‑AA extension readout is a key check‑in on that thesis, but it does not yet change the fact that the near term catalyst remains Phase 3 initiation and execution in atopic dermatitis, while the biggest risk is continued heavy losses without a clear path to commercialization.

The most closely linked recent announcement is Nektar’s plan to start the ZENITH‑AD Phase 3 program for rezpegaldesleukin in moderate‑to‑severe atopic dermatitis in Q2 2026. Together with the updated REZOLVE‑AA data, this positions REZPEG’s dermatology profile as the central driver of sentiment around Nektar, tying clinical progress in both indications to future funding needs, potential partnerships, and how investors weigh dilution risk against upside from a successful late‑stage program.

Yet while enthusiasm is building, investors should also understand how continued losses and the risk of further dilution could still weigh on Nektar’s story...

data.title: Does Tiger Brands (JSE:TBS) Deserve A Spot On Your Watchlist?
data.description:
Investors are often guided by the idea of discovering 'the next big thing', even if that means buying 'story stocks' without any revenue, let alone profit. But the reality is that when a company loses money each year, for long enough, its investors will usually take their share of those losses. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

In contrast to all that, many investors prefer to focus on companies like Tiger Brands (JSE:TBS), which has not only revenues, but also profits. Even if this company is fairly valued by the market, investors would agree that generating consistent profits will continue to provide Tiger Brands with the means to add long-term value to shareholders.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Tiger Brands' Earnings Per Share Are Growing

The market is a voting machine in the short term, but a weighing machine in the long term, so you'd expect share price to follow earnings per share (EPS) outcomes eventually. That means EPS growth is considered a real positive by most successful long-term investors. Tiger Brands managed to grow EPS by 16% per year, over three years. That's a pretty good rate, if the company can sustain it.

Careful consideration of revenue growth and earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margins can help inform a view on the sustainability of the recent profit growth. The music to the ears of Tiger Brands shareholders is that EBIT margins have grown from 8.4% to 11% in the last 12 months and revenues are on an upwards trend as well. Ticking those two boxes is a good sign of growth, in our book.

You can take a look at the company's revenue and earnings growth trend, in the chart below. Click on the chart to see the exact numbers.

Check out our latest analysis for Tiger Brands

Of course the knack is to find stocks that have their best days in the future, not in the past. You could base your opinion on past performance, of course, but you may also want to check this interactive graph of professional analyst EPS forecasts for Tiger Brands.

Are Tiger Brands Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

It's pleasing to see company leaders with putting their money on the line, so to speak, because it increases alignment of incentives between the people running the business, and its true owners. Tiger Brands followers will find comfort in knowing that insiders have a significant amount of capital that aligns their best interests with the wider shareholder group. With a whopping R1.4b worth of shares as a group, insiders have plenty riding on the company's success. This would indicate that the goals of shareholders and management are one and the same.

While it's always good to see some strong conviction in the company from insiders through heavy investment, it's also important for shareholders to ask if management compensation policies are reasonable. A brief analysis of the CEO compensation suggests they are. Our analysis has discovered that the median total compensation for the CEOs of companies like Tiger Brands with market caps between R33b and R105b is about R31m.

The CEO of Tiger Brands only received R15m in total compensation for the year ending September 2025. First impressions seem to indicate a compensation policy that is favourable to shareholders. CEO remuneration levels are not the most important metric for investors, but when the pay is modest, that does support enhanced alignment between the CEO and the ordinary shareholders. It can also be a sign of a culture of integrity, in a broader sense.

Is Tiger Brands Worth Keeping An Eye On?

As previously touched on, Tiger Brands is a growing business, which is encouraging. The fact that EPS is growing is a genuine positive for Tiger Brands, but the pleasant picture gets better than that. With company insiders aligning themselves considerably with the company's success and modest CEO compensation, there's no arguments that this is a stock worth looking into. We should say that we've discovered 4 warning signs for Tiger Brands (1 is significant!) that you should be aware of before investing here.

There's always the possibility of doing well buying stocks that are not growing earnings and do not have insiders buying shares. But for those who consider these important metrics, we encourage you to check out companies that do have those features. You can access a tailored list of South African companies which have demonstrated growth backed by significant insider holdings.

Please note the insider transactions discussed in this article refer to reportable transactions in the relevant jurisdiction.

data.title: We Think You Should Be Aware Of Some Concerning Factors In Vesuvius India's (NSE:VESUVIUS) Earnings
data.description:
Vesuvius India Limited (NSE:VESUVIUS) just released a solid earnings report, and the stock displayed some strength. However, we think that shareholders should be cautious as we found some worrying factors underlying the profit.

How Do Unusual Items Influence Profit?

To properly understand Vesuvius India's profit results, we need to consider the ₹251m gain attributed to unusual items. While it's always nice to have higher profit, a large contribution from unusual items sometimes dampens our enthusiasm. When we crunched the numbers on thousands of publicly listed companies, we found that a boost from unusual items in a given year is often not repeated the next year. And, after all, that's exactly what the accounting terminology implies. If Vesuvius India doesn't see that contribution repeat, then all else being equal we'd expect its profit to drop over the current year.

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of Vesuvius India.

Our Take On Vesuvius India's Profit Performance

We'd posit that Vesuvius India's statutory earnings aren't a clean read on ongoing productivity, due to the large unusual item. Because of this, we think that it may be that Vesuvius India's statutory profits are better than its underlying earnings power. But the good news is that its EPS growth over the last three years has been very impressive. The goal of this article has been to assess how well we can rely on the statutory earnings to reflect the company's potential, but there is plenty more to consider. While earnings are important, another area to consider is the balance sheet. If you're interested we have a graphic representation of Vesuvius India's balance sheet.
